summary: We have lived in St. Augustine, Florida, for thirty years now, settling into a house we eventually bought after renting for some time. This city has become our permanent home, offering us economic security and comfort. Much of my work in insurance and community involvement takes place here, especially in the colored settlement where I make my collection rounds. St. Augustine's neighborhoods, like Central Avenue, South Street, and Riberia Street, are familiar to me through my daily visits to clients.

My early boyhood was spent in Candler, Florida, a small place just a few miles from Ocala. It was there that my roots began before I moved on to other parts of the state and beyond. Though I left Candler behind, it remains an important part of my personal history and upbringing.

Ocala, Florida, was close to where I grew up, near the small town of Candler. While I didn't live directly in Ocala, its proximity shaped my early experiences before I moved on to other places like Augusta and St. Augustine. Ocala represents part of the backdrop of my youth in Florida.

During the Spanish–American War, I enlisted for service and trained at Fort Anderson in Alabama. I was underage at the time, so my mother arranged for my discharge. Though I never saw combat, that period marked my first experience with military life and discipline.

Fort Anderson, Alabama, was where I trained during the Spanish–American War. It was my first military experience, though brief because I was underage and had to be discharged. The time there gave me a glimpse of army life before I returned home.

My first job in insurance was with the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company, where I worked for five years in Augusta, Georgia. This was an important stepping stone in my career and life, as I also got married while living in Augusta. Metropolitan Life gave me the foundation I needed before moving on to a long career with another firm.

Augusta, Georgia, was my hometown and where I spent significant years working for Metropolitan Life Insurance Company. It was also where I got married and began my adult life. Though I later moved to St. Augustine, Augusta remains a key place in my personal and professional history.

My family and I have been affiliated with the Methodist Church for many years, taking an active interest in its work and organizations. I also contribute musically as a violinist, which is appreciated by the choir and congregation. The church has been a meaningful part of our community and personal life in St. Augustine.

I have been with the Peninsular Insurance Company for thirty-six years, earning the rating of senior agent. This long tenure reflects my dedication and experience in the insurance business, especially working with small weekly payment policies in the Negro community of St. Augustine. The company and I have grown together as I managed collections and built trust with my clients.

About three years ago, the Florida Legislature passed a law that effectively abolished burial contracts, changing how insurance claims were handled. This law meant that claims are now paid directly to beneficiaries, ending the old system with its sometimes peculiar burial provisions. The change simplified my work as an insurance agent and affected many of my clients in St. Augustine.

Central Avenue in St. Augustine is part of the area where I make my insurance collection rounds. I remember visiting number 15 Central Avenue, where despite the modest and shabby appearance, the residents took great pride in their homes and gardens. This street is typical of the neighborhoods I serve, reflecting the hardworking and thrifty nature of my clients.

The San Sebastian River marks the eastern boundary of the section where I collect insurance premiums in St. Augustine. On Riberia Street, near the riverbank, I visited a sick policyholder and saw firsthand the importance of the sick benefit policies for families facing hardship. The river area is part of the community I serve and know well.

South Street in St. Augustine is another part of the neighborhood where I conduct my insurance collections. I recall visiting a small, shabby clapboard house there, where despite the modest surroundings, the residents maintained beautiful flower gardens. South Street exemplifies the resilience and pride of the people I work with daily.

Riberia Street is part of the area along the San Sebastian River where I make my insurance collection calls. I remember visiting a small three-room house there where an elderly woman was ill but still cherished a quilt given to her years ago. This street and its residents are representative of the close-knit community I serve in St. Augustine.
